FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline 2016–2018, ages 9–10 years, N=10,317, Year 2 follow-up (longitudinal)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; logistic regression examining associations between ACEs and cyberbullying victimization

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published paper, Journal of Adolescence, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization is the DV; cyberbullying alone does not qualify per binding clarification: 'Cyberbullying alone fails IC2.' No phone/SM-specific variable with its own coefficient paired with individual-trait IV (ACEs is individual-trait-level, but cyberbullying is not a phone/SM modality variable)

notes: Paper examines ACEs (trauma exposure) as predictor of cyberbullying victimization. Cyberbullying is a behavior/outcome, not a phone/SM use variable. IC2 binding rule: cyberbullying alone fails regardless of paired predictor type.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018–2020), ages 10–14 years, N=9,443, cross-sectional

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; modified Poisson regression examining associations between cyberbullying victimization/perpetration and sleep disturbance

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying (victimization and perpetration) is the IV; sleep disturbance is the DV. Cyberbullying alone fails IC2 per binding clarification: 'Cyberbullying alone fails IC2.' No phone/SM-specific variable with separate coefficient. Cyberbullying is not a phone/SM use variable.

notes: Paper examines cyberbullying victimization and perpetration as predictors of sleep disturbance (trouble falling/staying asleep, caregiver-reported sleep disturbance). Cyberbullying is a social behavior outcome, not a phone/SM use variable. IC2 binding rule applies.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018–2020), ages 10–14 years, N=10,339, cross-sectional

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; multiple linear regression examining associations between sexual orientation and screen use (time and problematic use)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published paper, Annals of Epidemiology, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate found. All results are on composite recreational screen time. Step 2 composite check: "recreational screen time" defined as sum of 8 modalities: multi-player gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing, watching/streaming movies/videos/TV. Composite includes TV (non-phone/SM ingredient) and unspecified video games without per-device/per-platform breakout. Methods state: "Recreational screen use for the following modalities was determined using adolescents' self-reported hours of use on a typical weekday and weekend: multi-player gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing the internet, and watching/streaming movies, videos, or TV." No separate phone-only or SM-only estimate provided; composite bundles TV with SM/phone. Per IC2 binding rule: multi-device aggregated time fails Step 2.

notes: Paper examines sexual orientation (IV; identity variable = individual-trait per v7) and screen use/problematic use (DV; composite). Composite "recreational screen time" includes TV, unspecified gaming, and phone/SM items without isolation. IC2 Step 2 fails: non-phone/non-SM ingredient (TV) bundled without separate breakout.
